narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one—76% chance of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students like you. Your LSAT score of 28 stood out as the biggest red flag, the strongest signal pulling toward failure because it sits well below what the model has seen succeed in passing the bar. On top of that, your Year 3 decile of 1.0—ranking in the bottom tenth of your school—reinforced this heavily, suggesting challenges in building momentum even later in your studies. Your Year 1 decile of 1.0 echoed that early struggle, making it the third-most influential factor tipping the scales toward not passing. With a middle family income, your undergraduate GPA of 2.5, and being Black, these pieces wove in as steady undercurrents toward failure, painting a picture of hurdles the model associates with bar struggles. Going part-time added another layer, as it often correlates with divided focus in the data. The one bright spot was your gender—female students in the patterns slightly pulled toward passing—but it wasn't enough to outweigh the rest. Together, these factors created a profile the model couldn't confidently back for bar success, leading to this tough call on your application. It's not a judgment on your potential or heart; it's just the story these numbers told. If you boost that LSAT or strengthen your academic deciles next time, the picture could shift dramatically. You've got this—keep pushing.
